title: NVIDIA Launches Blackwell Ultra GPU: Doubles AI Training Performance Over H100
text:
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) unveiled its next-generation Blackwell Ultra GPU architecture at the company's annual GPU Technology Conference (GTC) in San Jose, California. CEO Jensen Huang delivered the keynote address to an audience of over 15,000 attendees, presenting what he called 'the engine of the next industrial revolution.'

The Blackwell Ultra B300 GPU, the flagship chip in the new lineup, delivers 2.4 petaflops of AI training performance, more than double the H100's throughput. The chip features 288GB of HBM3e memory with 8TB/s bandwidth, addressing one of the key bottlenecks in training large language models. NVLink 5 connectivity allows up to 576 GPUs to be interconnected in a single cluster with a combined memory pool of 165TB.

NVIDIA announced that major cloud providers including Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, and Oracle Cloud have already committed to deploying Blackwell Ultra-based instances. AWS CEO Matt Garman appeared on stage to announce that the new UltraCluster configurations would be available to customers starting Q3 2026.

The launch comes at a critical time when demand for AI compute infrastructure has reached unprecedented levels. NVIDIA's data center revenue grew 409% year-over-year in fiscal 2025, and analysts expect continued strong growth with the Blackwell Ultra transition. Wall Street's consensus revenue estimate for fiscal 2027 (ending January 2027) is $185 billion, implying further acceleration from fiscal 2026's $130 billion.

Huang also unveiled the NIM (NVIDIA Inference Microservices) platform expansion, allowing enterprises to deploy optimized AI models with a single API call. Over 100 enterprise software companies have already integrated NIM into their products, including Salesforce, ServiceNow, and SAP.

On the competition front, NVIDIA faces challenges from AMD's MI400 series, Intel's Gaudi 4, and Google's custom TPU v6 chips, as well as Amazon's Trainium chips. However, NVIDIA's software ecosystem, particularly the CUDA platform and extensive developer tools, continues to provide a significant competitive moat.

NVDA shares rose 8.4% on the day of the announcement, adding approximately $220 billion in market capitalization. The stock has been one of the best performers in the S&P 500 over the past three years, returning over 780% since January 2023. Analysts at Goldman Sachs maintained their Buy rating with a $1,000 price target, noting that 'the Blackwell Ultra launch solidifies NVIDIA's leadership position for at least the next 18-24 months.'

The company also announced a 10-for-1 stock split effective March 15, 2026, following the pattern of the 2024 split that made shares more accessible to retail investors.
